steps.message:
You are an AI assistant tasked with solving command-line tasks in a Linux environment. You will be given a task description and the output from previously executed commands. Your goal is to solve the task by providing batches of shell commands.

Format your response as JSON with the following structure:

{
  "analysis": "Analyze the current state based on the terminal output provided. What do you see? What has been accomplished? What still needs to be done?",
  "plan": "Describe your plan for the next steps. What commands will you run and why? Be specific about what you expect each command to accomplish.",
  "commands": [
    {
      "keystrokes": "ls -la\n",
      "duration": 0.1
    },
    {
      "keystrokes": "cd project\n",
      "duration": 0.1
    }
  ],
  "task_complete": true
}

Required fields:
- "analysis": Your analysis of the current situation
- "plan": Your plan for the next steps
- "commands": Array of command objects to execute

Optional fields:
- "task_complete": Boolean indicating if the task is complete (defaults to false if not present)

Command object structure:
- "keystrokes": String containing the exact keystrokes to send to the terminal (required)
- "duration": Number of seconds to wait for the command to complete before the next command will be executed (defaults to 1.0 if not present)

IMPORTANT: The text inside "keystrokes" will be used completely verbatim as keystrokes. Write commands exactly as you want them sent to the terminal:
- You must end every command with a newline (\n) or it will not execute.
- For special key sequences, use tmux-style escape sequences:
  - C-c for Ctrl+C
  - C-d for Ctrl+D

The "duration" attribute specifies the number of seconds to wait for the command to complete (default: 1.0) before the next command will be executed. On immediate tasks (e.g., cd, ls, echo, cat) set a duration of 0.1 seconds. On commands (e.g., gcc, find, rustc) set a duration of 1.0 seconds. On slow commands (e.g., make, python3 [long running script], wget [file]) set an appropriate duration as you determine necessary.

It is better to set a smaller duration than a longer duration. It is always possible to wait again if the prior output has not finished, by running {"keystrokes": "", "duration": 10.0} on subsequent requests to wait longer. Never wait longer than 60 seconds; prefer to poll to see intermediate result status.

Important notes:
- Each command's keystrokes are sent exactly as written to the terminal
- Do not include extra whitespace before or after the keystrokes unless it's part of the intended command
- Extra text before or after the JSON will generate warnings but be tolerated
- The JSON must be valid - use proper escaping for quotes and special characters within strings
- Commands array can be empty if you want to wait without taking action

Task Description:
The last piece is the browser client in /app/www/app.js. Point fetch at the same-origin API base /api/notes. When the script runs, load the list into the notes-list element. Each row needs edit and delete buttons whose class lists include edit-btn and delete-btn.

Submitting note-form should create a note when nothing is selected and update the active row otherwise. When create or save succeeds, note-title and note-content should be empty and the form should be ready for a new note on the next submit rather than staying on the previous row. Delete must call the API and refresh the list. After every successful POST, PUT, or DELETE, reload the list so the page matches the database.

Send Content-Type application/json and the trimmed API key from /app/config/api.key on POST, PUT, and DELETE only, not on GET. The automated frontend harness is not a real browser; read /app/docs/js_verifier_mock.md for DOM and fetch quirks before you declare done. Keep /app.js reachable over HTTP from the running server on port 8080, not only on disk.


Current terminal state:
New Terminal Output:

echo revived

Analysis: We need to implement the browser client logic in /app/www/app.js per requirements: same-origin fetch base /api/notes; load notes into #notes-list on script run; render rows with edit/delete buttons having classes edit-btn/delete-btn; form submit creates or updates depending on selected note; after successful create/update clear inputs and reset selection for next submit; delete calls API and refreshes list; after every successful POST/PUT/DELETE reload list. Must send Content-Type: application/json and trimmed API key from /app/config/api.key on POST/PUT/DELETE only (not GET). Also must consider DOM/fetch quirks per /app/docs/js_verifier_mock.md. Ensure /app.js is served from server on port 8080. Next: inspect docs and existing app.js, implement accordingly, then verify via curl that server serves /app.js and (optionally) run any tests.
Plan: 1) Inspect /app/docs/js_verifier_mock.md to understand harness quirks. 2) Open current /app/www/app.js to see existing structure and required DOM IDs. 3) Read API key file. 4) Implement robust client: state for activeNoteId; loadNotes() on startup; render list with data attributes; event delegation for edit/delete; form submit handler does POST or PUT; on success clear fields and active selection; delete handler does DELETE then reload. Include headers only for POST/PUT/DELETE. 5) Ensure API base is '/api/notes'. 6) Quick sanity check by grepping for required class names and base URL. 7) Confirm server on 8080 serves /app.js with curl.

Analysis: The previous command batch started printing /app/www/app.js but the terminal output shown is truncated mid-file, and we haven’t yet seen js_verifier_mock.md content, the API key, the rest of app.js, nor the results of grep/curl. We need to (1) read js_verifier_mock.md for any required quirks, (2) review the remainder of app.js to ensure no syntax issues and all requirements met, (3) verify the config key path is actually served at /config/api.key (or adjust if needed), and (4) confirm the running server on :8080 serves /app.js and that the client won’t break in the harness (notably around closest(), dataset, classList, event delegation).
Plan: 1) Re-display js_verifier_mock.md to catch any DOM/fetch constraints that require code changes. 2) Show the rest of /app/www/app.js to confirm it’s complete and syntactically valid. 3) Check whether /config/api.key is reachable over HTTP from localhost:8080; if not, adjust key loading to the correct served path (or embed key another way consistent with requirements). 4) Run greps/curl again to confirm API base and class names and that /app.js is served.

Analysis: Only a solitary '+' was returned, which strongly suggests the terminal/session is currently stuck in a continued input state (e.g., an unclosed heredoc/apply_patch, an open quote, or a pager) rather than running the requested commands. We need to regain a normal shell prompt before proceeding.
Plan: Send Ctrl+C to cancel any pending multiline input and return to the shell prompt. Then run a trivial command to confirm the shell is responsive.

results.content:
Previous response had warnings:
WARNINGS: - Command 1 should end with newline when followed by another command. Otherwise the two commands will be concatenated together on the same line.

New Terminal Output:

+

steps.message:
Analysis: The session still appears stuck in a multiline input state (we only see '+'). The prior Ctrl+C likely did not get sent correctly due to missing newline sequencing; also the system warned about not ending the keystrokes with a newline, so the control sequence may have been concatenated. We need to send Ctrl+C cleanly (with newline after) and, if needed, Ctrl+D to exit any open input mode.
Plan: 1) Send Ctrl+C with a newline to interrupt. 2) If still stuck, send Ctrl+D with newline to end input. 3) Verify we’re back at a shell prompt by running an echo.

Analysis: Still receiving only '+', meaning we’re likely inside a program that uses '+' as a continuation prompt (common in some REPLs) or the terminal is wedged in a state where input is not being executed as shell commands. We should try additional interrupts and then request a fresh shell by exiting the current process.
Plan: 1) Send multiple interrupts (Ctrl+C) to try to break out. 2) Send 'exit' to terminate a subshell/REPL if it accepts it. 3) If that fails, send Ctrl+\ (SIGQUIT) as a stronger termination signal. 4) Finally run 'pwd' to confirm we are back in the shell.

Analysis: We are still stuck with only '+'. This suggests the interactive program isn't responding to signals (or they aren’t being delivered), and we can’t reliably proceed within this terminal session. The most practical path is to start a fresh non-interactive command execution context by running a new login shell via tmux/new session isn’t available here, but we can attempt to spawn a clean shell using /bin/bash -lc. If keystrokes are still being routed to the stuck process, even that won’t work; in that case the environment likely requires a reset by the harness, but we can at least attempt to force an exec of a new shell.
Plan: Try to launch a new shell process explicitly. If we can get it to run, it will execute commands regardless of the prior stuck state. We’ll run /bin/bash -lc with a simple echo to validate, then continue with the pending inspections.

Analysis: All keystrokes are still being consumed by the same stuck interactive prompt (printing '+'), so we cannot execute any further shell commands in this session. Without the ability to run commands, we can’t verify or adjust /app/www/app.js or confirm HTTP serving on :8080. The environment needs the terminal session reset/restarted by the harness/controller.
Plan: Attempt one last universal break sequence (Ctrl+C, Ctrl+D) and then send an empty line. If it remains stuck, the only next step is to request a session reset/new terminal from the controller.

Analysis: The terminal is irrecoverably stuck in a continuation prompt state (always returning '+') and is not accepting interrupts or commands. This prevents completing verification and any required fixes. The remaining work depends on being able to run shell commands, so we need a fresh terminal session.
Plan: No further commands can be executed in the current session. A new terminal/session (or environment reset) is required to proceed.

Analysis: Still stuck at '+'. No commands can run in this terminal session.
Plan: Wait. If a new terminal/session becomes available, we can continue. Otherwise the controller must reset the shell.

Analysis: The shell remains stuck showing only '+', indicating the session cannot execute commands. We cannot complete or verify the required changes without a functioning shell.
Plan: No actionable commands in this session. The controller needs to provide a fresh terminal session/reset.

Analysis: The terminal session is still stuck and non-functional (only '+' output). Command execution is not possible, so progress is blocked.
Plan: Stop sending commands; require a new session/reset from the harness to proceed.

Plan: No commands can be run successfully in this session. A fresh terminal/session reset is required to proceed.
